items.question: Explain split-apply-combine with a pandas groupby example.
items.answer: Split: rows partitioned by key such as customer_id. Apply: a function like sum or mean runs per group. Combine: results stack into a Series or DataFrame indexed by the key. Example: orders.groupby('customer_id')['amount'].sum() returns total spend per customer.

items.question: When would you use how='left' vs how='inner' in pd.merge?
items.answer: Use left when every row in the left (fact) table must survive—unmatched dimension keys become NaN. Use inner when only rows with matches in both tables are valid, accepting that unmatched left rows are dropped. Always compare len(merged) to len(left) after inner joins.

items.question: What is the difference between agg and transform after groupby?
items.answer: agg collapses each group to summary statistics—one row per group key—suitable for entity-level feature tables. transform returns values aligned to the original index, broadcasting group statistics to each row—useful for normalization or row-level deviations within group.

items.question: How can a groupby-merge pipeline cause data leakage?
items.answer: Computing group statistics on the full dataset before train/test split lets test rows influence features mapped back to themselves. Merging label or post-outcome tables before splitting leaks target information. Fix: split first, aggregate on train only, map to test; keep labels separate until evaluation boundaries are set.

items.question: How do you detect an unintended many-to-many merge?
items.answer: Compare row count before and after merge—unexpected growth signals duplicate keys. Check df[key].duplicated().any() on both sides. Use merge(..., indicator=True) to inspect match categories and validate='m:1' or '1:1' when you expect at most one right row per left key.
